games.explanation: Cam Schlittler is being treated like a problem the Royals can't solve, and the numbers underneath suggest the opposite. Against right-handed bats he's been distinctly hittable, with a barrel rate near ten percent, and Kansas City's top of the order (Witt, Perez, Garcia) is built to do exactly that kind of damage from the right side. Falter is no prize either, but plus-money on a coin flip against a Yankees rotation arm with command questions is the entire pitch. At +170, you're getting paid to bet on the lineup that actually shows up against this pitcher type.

Jack Leiter's ERA suggests a pitcher figuring it out, but the underlying profile is still a guy who walks the ballpark and gets barreled when he's in the zone. Houston's right-handed core is built to exploit exactly that, and Leiter has been notably worse against righties, where his wOBA-against sits at .364 and the walks pile up fastest. Alexander isn't doing anything special on the other side, but he doesn't have to in a game the board is treating like a coin flip on a team that's the clear favorite by Elo. Getting plus money on the better roster against a command-deficient righty is the entire pitch, and +115 is generous for it.

Kyle Freeland on the road against this lineup is the kind of assignment the Rockies keep handing him because they have no other option, and the market is still pricing it like a normal heavy favorite spot. Freeland gives up hard contact to right-handed bats at a near-50% clip, which is the exact profile Ohtani and Freeman feast on, and Coors-adjacent power plays just fine when the visitor brings the better hitters. Lauer is the quieter half of this story but he has been the sharper arm by xwOBA, so the run prevention side holds up too. At -230 you are not getting a bargain, you are getting a number that should be closer to -270.

Falter is no prize either, but plus-money on a coin flip against a Yankees rotation arm with command questions is the entire pitch. At +170, you're getting paid to bet on the lineup that actually shows up against this pitcher type.

The Yankees are favored to win this game, and they probably will, but the board is pricing it like a comfortable Bronx evening instead of the scrappy one-run grind it's more likely to be. Schlittler punishes lefties and has been far more vulnerable to right-handed bats, which is exactly the side Kansas City stacks with Witt, Garcia, and Perez at the top. Falter is no prize, but the sim lands on a 4.9-5.5 finish, the kind of game where +1.5 cashes even when the favorite gets the W. Getting plus money on a cover line in a projected one-run fight is the entire reason to take the dog's runs.

Getting plus money on the better roster against a command-deficient righty is the entire pitch, and +115 is generous for it.
